title: Years After The War In Australia
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Big rough boys from the runs out back were first where the balls flew free,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yelled in the slang of the Outside Track: ן¿½By God, itן¿½s a Christmas spree!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s not too rustyן¿½, and ן¿½Wool away!ן¿½, ן¿½stand clear of the blazing shoots!ן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sheep O! Sheep O!ן¿½, ן¿½Weן¿½ll cut out to-dayן¿½, ן¿½Look out for the bossן¿½s boots!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What price the tally in camp to-night!ן¿½, ן¿½What price the boys Out Back!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go it, you tigers, for Right or Might and the pride of the Outside Track!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Needle and thread!ן¿½, ן¿½I have broke my comb!ן¿½, ן¿½Now ride, you flour-bags, ride!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Fight for your mates and the folk at home!ן¿½, ן¿½Hereן¿½s for the Lachlan side!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those men of the West would sneer and scoff at the gates of hell ajar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And oft the sight of a head cut off was hailed by a yell for ן¿½Tar!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I heard the push in the Red Redoubt, irate at a luckless shot:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Look out for the blooming shell, look out!ן¿½, ן¿½Gorן¿½ bliן¿½me, but thatן¿½s red-hot!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s Bill the Slogger, poor bloke, heן¿½s done. A chunk of the shell was his;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I wish the be beggar that fired that gun could get within reach of Liz.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Those foreign gunners will give us rats, but I wish it was Bill they missed.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½d like to get at their bleeding hats with a rock in my (something) fist.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hold up, Billy; Iן¿½ll stick to you; theyן¿½ve hit you under the belt;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If we get the waddle Iן¿½ll swag you through, if the blazing mountains melt;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You remember the night when the traps got me for stoushing a bleeding Chow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And you went for ן¿½em proper and laid out three, and I wonן¿½t forget it now.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, groaning and swearing, the pug replied: ן¿½Iן¿½m done . . . theyן¿½ve knocked me out!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½d fight them all for a pound a-side, from the boss to the rouseabout.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My nut is cracked and my legs is broke, and it gives me worse than hell;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I trained for a scrap with a twelve-stone bloke, and not with a bursting shell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You neednן¿½t mag, for I knowed, old chum, I knowed, old pal, youן¿½d stick;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But you canן¿½t hold out till the regן¿½lars come, and youן¿½d best be nowhere quick.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Theyן¿½ve got a force and a gun ashore, both of our wings is broke;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Theyן¿½ll storm the ridge in a minute more, and the best you can do is smoke.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Jim exclaimed: ן¿½You can smoke, you chaps, but me, Gorן¿½ bliן¿½me, no!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The push that ran from the George-street traps wonן¿½t run from a foreign foe.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll stick to the gun while she makes them sick, and Iן¿½ll stick to whatן¿½s left of Bill.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they hiss through their blackened teeth: ן¿½Weן¿½ll stick! by the blazing flame, we will!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And long years after the war was past, they told in the town and bush
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How the ridge of death to the bloody last was held by a Sydney push;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How they fought to the end in a sheet of flame, how they fought with their rifle-stocks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And earned, in a nobler sense, the name of their ancient weapons, ן¿½rocks.ן¿½


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the western camps it was ever our boast, when ן¿½twas bad for the kangaroo:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If the enemyן¿½s forces take the coast, they must take the mountains, too;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They may force their way by the western line or round by a northern track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they wonן¿½t run short of a decent spree with the men who are left out back!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When we burst the enemyן¿½s ironclads and won by a run of luck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We whooped as loudly as Nelsonן¿½s lads when a French three-decker struck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when the enemyן¿½s troops prevailed the truth was never heard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We lied like heroes who never failed explaining how that occurred.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You bushmen sneer in the old bush way at the new-chum jackeroo,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But ן¿½cuffs-ן¿½nן¿½-collersן¿½ were out that day, and they stuck to their posts like glue;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I never believed that a dude could fight till a Johnny led us then;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We buried his bits in the rear that night for the honour of George-street men.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Jim the Ringer, he fought, he did. The regiment nicknamed Jim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Old Heads a Caserן¿½ and ן¿½Heads a Quid,ן¿½ but it never was ן¿½tailsן¿½ with him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The way that he rode was a racing rhyme, and the way that he finished grand;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He backed the enemy every time, and died in a hand-to-hand!


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll never forget when the ringer and I were first in the Bush Brigade,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Warrego Bill, from the Live-till-you-Die, in the last grand charge we made.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Billy died, he was full of sand, he said, as I raised his head:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½m full of love for my native land, but a lot too full of lead.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tell ן¿½em,ן¿½ said Billy, ן¿½and tell old dad, to look after the cattle pup;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But his eyes grew bright, though his voice was sad, and he said, as I held him up:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I have been happy on western farms. And once, when I first went wrong,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Around my neck were the trembling arms of the girl Iן¿½d loved so long.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Far out on the southern seas Iן¿½ve sailed, and ridden where brumbies roam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And oft, when all on the station failed, Iן¿½ve driven the outlaw home.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ve spent a cheque in a day and night, and Iן¿½ve made a cheque as quick;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I struck a nugget when times were tight, and the stores had stopped our tick.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ve led the field on the old bay mare, and I hear the cheering still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When mother and sister and she were there, and the old man yelled for Bill;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But, save for her, could I live my while again in the old bush way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½d give it all for the last half-mile in the race we rode to-day!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he passed away as the stars came out, he died as old heroes die,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I heard the sound of the distant rout, and the Southern Cross was high.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
